segments.text: Thank you so much.
Thank you for the wait. No problem. Yeah. Um, thank you.
So, um, it's a joint hearing with, uh, DIA
and Public Service Commission.
So if you would like to start with some opening remarks,
you want to do it, um,
in your individual organization capacity
or together, that's totally fine.
Thank you. And then we'll go onto questions. Thank you.
Uh, thank you, um, Madam Chair
and committee, and, uh, I'll be brief.
My name's Paul James. I'm the Government Chief Digital
Officer and the Chief
Executive of Department of Internal Affairs.
Um, and it's a pleasure to be here.
I'm joined by Murray Davy, um,
who is the Chief Digital Officer for Department
of Internal Affairs, and my colleague
from the Public Services Commission.
But you introduce yourself, Hugo.
Um, thank you, Madam Chair. My name's Hugo Batal.
I'm the Deputy Chief Executive Strategy, policy
and Integrity at the commission.
Thank you. And so in terms
of just some brief introductory remarks, um, so
as Government Chief Digital Officer are mandated
by the Public Services Commission under Public Services
Legislation, uh, as a system lead,
and that means that I have responsibility
to work across the public service, not just down
inside my own agency, um, across digital.
So practically what that looks like is we have a focus on
helping ensure there's a consistent vision
and direction for digital across the public sector.
Uh, we work around standards, policies
and guidance to help consistency
and coherence across the public sector.
And we also put in place tools
and support services to support the public sector.
We don't take away from individual agency
or see accountability, uh, to deliver the services
and take responsibility for their own digital, uh, service
and systems and applications.
But we look to provide value in a collective.
And so, uh, for example, uh, we have a, uh, collection
of services, things like, uh, a cloud framework agreement
with a major company.
So public service agencies come in, uh,
through our commercial constructs to access those services.
It means that we scale up the purchasing power of the crown
through those contracts
and get, um, benefits around pricing,
but we can also put in, um, standards around security
and other things that are important like that.
So that's a system lead role.
And in terms of ai, I've also been asked to lead, um, uh,
the adoption of AI for the public service.
And so take a similar approach to what we do around digital,
but with ai, which there's clearly close connections.
And so, um, my focus is on the adoption
of AI by the public service.
So, uh, the customer base for us is the public service.
So that's who we focus on as agencies.
Of course, we think deeply about New Zealanders
because that's the public services customers.
Um, but our starting point is the agencies that we work
with, and it focuses around the adoption of ai.
I didn't wanna say a lot more than that.
I thought that, uh, it would be best to be guided
by the committee in
terms of the questions that you might have.
Great. Thank you. Anything that you would like
to add? Um, hug,
Uh, no, uh, the only two other elements other than, uh,
the designation of, um,
the government Chief Digital Officer under the Public
Service Act, uh, the commission, uh, is also, uh,
obviously the, um, employer on behalf of the Crown
of Chief Executives also has a responsibility
for developing capability of its, of its leaders.
And we do that through the Leadership development Center,
which is club funded by, uh, all agencies.
Uh, and then, um, you know, we, uh,
also have responsibilities around performance
and accountability and, um,
and for, uh, integrity and ethics.
So promoting, uh, account, uh, promoting integrity, uh,
transparency and accountability, that's,
that's our main areas for,
that are relevant for the committee today.
That's great. Thank you, Dr. Hamish.
Yeah. Excellent. Um, thank you.
I appreciate talking about the adoption of AI
and public service.
How much of it is actually being driven down,
uh, from, from top?
And how much is actually people
actually developing these tools
and using the tools, whether it be, um, bundled
with other, other software?
That's a great question. I think it's
really important that we have both.
Uh, why it's really important we have both is that, uh,
like any tool, um, AI needs to be applied
to what's the objective here?
What's the business purpose, what's the benefit?
What are we trying to achieve?
And that will, uh, most often come from up, if you like,
from down from people who are working,
have accountabilities, responsibilities.
Uh, but what we also know when there's new technology coming
in and the public service needs to respond

by themselves, totally a new
and a fresh, how can we actually accelerate
or support by bringing in consistent standards, templates,

Um, over time, I'd expect us to mature a little bit too.
And by that I mean we'll start putting in place common
tools, some common technology solutions for people to use,
but at the moment, we're not at that point.
Mm-hmm. So, yeah.
Do, do you have any other technologies which this
approach has been used to, um, for its adoption and the
Public service?
Yeah, I mean, this is how we work generally
across, across digital.
So we try and we put in place frameworks, expectations,
common approaches,
but agencies know
their responsibilities better than anyone else.
They've got their own accountabilities.
Um, you know, I'm not gonna be
responsible for social services.
That's gonna be the responsibility
of the Ministry of Social Development.
Um, and technology needs to be deployed in context

and to improve a New Zealanders experience or outcomes.
And so it's getting the balance between
how do you have something that is coherent across the public
service, but is actually responsive to the immediate needs

and the new Zealanders that they support
Who've been doing. Thank you. Um,
I'm curious

broadly the 46 core agencies that are our mandate.

Uh, we've just got several hundred people, uh, come online

And so in that conversation, we get a sense as well.
So it is, as we see across other technologies mixed in terms

Um, A, CC
and IR are quite, um, um, proactive
and they're quite engaged in ai, uh, themselves.
And there are other organizations.
It tends to reflect their digital maturity as well.
Either their data isn't structural set up
or they're not cloud hosting,
and it's harder to start to deploy the technologies.
So they are earlier in terms of what they might be doing.
So we see variety at the moment, but I'd identify a, C, C
and IR as two organizations that are more advanced in their,
um, exploration of the value or utility of
Ai. Yeah, sure. And
at the opposite end of the spectrum,
the lower uptake or the, the least use,

But, um, for some organizations, um, perhaps, uh,
technology is a bigger part of their, uh, business.
Maybe some of the population agencies, agencies

So we are really encouraging people.

Prepare for that. And often it's just about your own

our public system.

Yes, absolutely.

that's definitely the case that you can see bias

deployment of AI into the public sector.
The one is very limited. So, um, it,
it's into administrative tasks

the modern AI tools being deployed into core

So we don't see that yet.

And it's all very much aimed at trying to make sure that,
uh, there's careful, responsible, ethical

So I think a lot of the risks can be seen if, um, you go

he'll be horrified if we just deployed AI straight into our

So it is creating the right technical environment,

and then making sure we've got all the right

So I, I think we should, uh, be mindful of the risks,

Mm-hmm. I might add, uh, there's also controls

So, uh, approaches like retrieval, augmenta,

augmented generation, or rag as it's called that enable you

So that helps you to make sure

So, um, so
That's More like a customized kind
